words: SAM SCHRAGER: You had mentioned to me that you had a chance to go overseas to
Tokyo.
LOUIS BOAS: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Now, you decided to go to Moscow instead?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, well, the man who I was working withAwas editor of the Idaho Statesman,

speaker: LB
words: it was aman by the name of Applegate. And he

speaker: LB
words: used to be professor of journalism at the University of Montana. And

speaker: LB
words: he had connections with people in Japan. And we talked late at night

speaker: LB
words: and he said he thought he could get me a job with the Tokyo Advertiser.

speaker: LB
words: And I said, "I think that would be interesting. I would like that."

speaker: LB
words: It never came down to the point where I had an offer, but he was star

speaker: LB
words: ting negotiations. Then I had a chance to come to Moscow and buy an

speaker: LB
words: interest in the paper here. So security was the reason why I came,

speaker: LB
words: rather than adventure of being overseas. And at that time one journa

speaker: LB
words: list from Boise was on the Tokyo Advertiser, Hallett Abend. And during

speaker: LB
words: World War II he was in the news quite a bit as an expert on Japan and

speaker: LB
words: he lectured in this country and so forth and he had come from Boise,

speaker: LB
words: also. So that was an idea to have fun and have adventure and so forth.

speaker: LB
words: The security of coming here and making some money was the reason why I

speaker: LB
words: chose it.

speaker: SS
words: When you first came here did you look it as a longterm commitment?

speaker: LB
words: Yeah, because I bought an interest in the paper. I had gone to school

speaker: LB
words: here so I was familiar with the town and I had worked on the paper parttime,

speaker: LB
words: so that I knew what it was and so forth. So I committed to make

speaker: LB
words: this my life's work.

speaker: SS
words: Iwanted to ask you who you remember in the '20's as being the prominent

speaker: SS
words: people in Moscow.

speaker: LB
words: In Moscow? Well, there was A. H. Oversmith, a lawyer. And George Lanphere, who

speaker: LB
words: was my partner and Haaken Melgaard. He owned what is now

speaker: LB
words: the First Security Bank. At that time it was a private bank, First

speaker: LB
words: Trust and Savings Bank. And he and his brother in Minnesota owned it

speaker: LB
words: lock,stock,and barrel. And he was quite prominent. And then Roland

speaker: LB
words: Hodgins, who ran a drugstore here; the drugstore still carries his

speaker: LB
words: name: Hodgins Drugstore. And he was very prominent. And then of

speaker: LB
words: course, F. A. David, who was the father of the David Brothers who

speaker: LB
words: own David's store. Frank A. David owned the store outright, it was

speaker: LB
words: just a family affair and the sons worked with him, of course. And

speaker: LB
words: in the 1920's- Oh, trying to think- Dr. Gritman was quite well known

speaker: LB
words: and quite popular. It is for him that Gritman Hospital is now named.

speaker: LB
words: And he owned a hospital that stood upon the grounds where Gritman Hos

speaker: LB
words: pital is now. It was a frame structure. He was quite prominent.

speaker: LB
words: I ve missed some, I know, but those are the ones^I can think of.

speaker: SS
words: How important were the newspapers in town at that time?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, I think it was important to the town. It was the only source of

speaker: LB
words: getting information through the town. We had no radio station then.

speaker: LB
words: Radio station didn't come until, gracious, in the 1940's, I believe.

speaker: LB
words: I would say we didn't have any radio station until about 1940, and it

speaker: LB
words: was the only way of getting information out, was through the newspaper.

speaker: LB
words: Much more so than now, I think, actually. And the items were small

speaker: LB
words: items, but they were important to the people of town. And it gave the

speaker: LB
words: merchants an opportunity to advertise. It was the only advertising

speaker: LB
words: media.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the local coverage of the paper was more important than

speaker: SS
words: the national at that time?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, definitely. Still is. I think more than half of your people in

speaker: LB
words: town take an outside paper, either from Spokane or Lewiston. Both the

speaker: LB
words: Spokesman Review and the Lewiston paper have large circulations here.

speaker: LB
words: And I think they take the local paper now for the local news, be

speaker: LB
words: cause they can get the world news in larger proportions through the

speaker: LB
words: Spokane or Lewiston publications.

speaker: SS
words: At that time?

speaker: LB
words: At that time and I think today.

speaker: SS
words: They were tak^ the Spokane paper?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, yes. Not so much in the early '20s. The Lewiston paper didn't

speaker: LB
words: have any circulation here but the Spokesman Review did. And more than

speaker: LB
words: half the people took it. And I am sure that more than half the people

speaker: LB
words: now take one or the other; the Spokesman or the Lewiston Tribune. But

speaker: LB
words: there's no other publication that serves the city and gives the city

speaker: LB
words: information. The Lewiston Tribune has some Moscow news and the Spokes

speaker: LB
words: man Review has some.

speaker: SS
words: But not much.

speaker: LB
words: But not as much, no. That's what your local paper is for is to stress

speaker: LB
words: the local news and people can take the outside paper orftake Time Ma

speaker: LB
words: gazine* or News_Week. But your local newspaper should be, and must

speaker: LB
words: be the conveyor of local information and serve as a watchdog over local

speaker: LB
words: government. That's No. 1.

speaker: SS
words: Did your paper give that?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: LB
words: In those days? In the early '20's?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, yes, surely. That's the major responsibility of a newspaper is to

speaker: LB
words: be the watchdog over public institutions as a representative of the

speaker: LB
words: people. That's why you have to constantly needle public officials.

speaker: LB
words: That's your job; keep them on their toes. No question about it. I've

speaker: LB
words: maintained for years that any newspaper that didn't fight wifrh the

speaker: LB
words: police department wasn't doing anything. Always. Because they like

speaker: LB
words: to hide things. That's natural. And your job was to fight 'em! I

speaker: LB
words: never let a reporter fight. If he has trouble getting something out

speaker: LB
words: of the police blotter or the city official, if he had trouble, he was

speaker: LB
words: to be quiet and calm and just accept it; tell me. And I'd go and do

speaker: LB
words: the fighting. Sometimes you had to use threats. Say, "Okay, if we

speaker: LB
words: don't get this, I'll call up a lawyer and you'll get a subpoena." So

speaker: LB
words: you'd break 'em down. Does that sound funny to you?

speaker: LB
words: No, it doesn't at all, because that seems to be the way it is now.

speaker: LB
words: That's what the newspapers are for. They are charged with that by the

speaker: LB
words: When

speaker: LB
words: constitution, ^the Constitution says the First Amendment: The

speaker: LB
words: people shall have a right to the free press and free speech. The peo

speaker: LB
words: ple have a right to a free press; not the newspaper. The newspaper

speaker: LB
words: has no more liberties than I have as an individual, but the people

speaker: LB
words: have the right to a free press. So that puts responsibility that free

speaker: LB
words: press work for the people. If you find corruption in city govenrment;

speaker: LB
words: expose it. Don't let's cover up. Many people think that the news

speaker: LB
words: papers are given a privilege under the constitution; they are not.

speaker: LB
words: It's the people that's given the privilege to have a free press. That's

speaker: LB
words: a great distinction.

speaker: SS
words: In a small town like Moscow, as it was then, in practical terms, it

speaker: SS
words: would seem to me that it would be difficult to have a town

speaker: SS
words: I mean to have too much of an adversary relationship within the powers

speaker: SS
words: of the town itself. I would think that there would be pressures, a

speaker: SS
words: lot of pressures on the paper not to look too closely/lnto the-

speaker: LB
words: There is, there is.

speaker: SS
words: Even in a small town like this, I wouldn't think that they could go

speaker: SS
words: too far.

speaker: LB
words: 0h» you've got to use restraint. There is always a threat of libel,

speaker: LB
words: you ve got to be factual; if you aren't you're going to get sued. And

speaker: LB
words: I've been sued twice. My paper's been sued twice for libel. Both

speaker: LB
words: times we were freed; in other words, two different times I was sued

speaker: LB
words: for $150,000 and the paper was sued for $150,000. We won both cases.

speaker: LB
words: Because the main thing is, the truth will prevail.

speaker: SS
words: TeH me briefly what basis the suits could be brought against you.

speaker: LB
words: Well, having libeled an individual. I'll tell you the second one:

speaker: LB
words: The first one was over a controversy with a Naval ROTC student and a

speaker: LB
words: man in town having created a disturbance and the paper got involved

speaker: LB
words: in it and we upheld the student's rights and there was a public meeting

speaker: LB
words: and so forth; I'll skip that. The second one is typical of how a news

speaker: LB
words: paper can get sued for libel and yet be innocent. A man came here a

speaker: LB
words: number of years ago with the idea of establishing a program- businessto

speaker: LB
words: provide programs for the radio stations of Idaho. Sort of a syndi

speaker: LB
words: cate idea. And he rented a building up on North Main Street, and he

speaker: LB
words: was going to use the talents of the University and Washington State

speaker: LB
words: College and hopefully to get national advertising for that program.

speaker: LB
words: Well, he was living at Coeur d'Alene, Hayden Lake, and the police got

speaker: LB
words: a telephone warrant for his arrest from Coeur d'Alene. and the charge

speaker: LB
words: was; passing bad checks. Well, we carried just a little item about an

speaker: LB
words: inch and a half long that he was arrested here in Moscow at the motel

speaker: LB
words: upon a warrant from Coeur d'Alene for passing bad checks. Well, the

speaker: LB
words: police came down from Coeur d'Alene and took him back, and I didn't

speaker: LB
words: even pay any attention to it. About five months later we were served

speaker: LB
words: a subpoena as being charged with libel, referred to this article. Gosh,

speaker: LB
words: I looked the article up and I couldn't see anything wrong with it, so

speaker: LB
words: I sent a reporter to the police station and they looked back in the

speaker: LB
words: log and found where it had been typed out, and our story was absolutely

speaker: LB
words: factual. But it turned out that he had only written one check and the

speaker: LB
words: police got it as checks, plural; we reported it as checks, plural. So

speaker: LB
words: we consulted our lawyer, we a lawyer from Lewiston, who was very

speaker: LB
words: good, Clemmons, and he said','That sounds bad." So he made a trip to

speaker: LB
words: Seattle to confer with the recognized libel expert^ the United States,

speaker: LB
words: He happened to^live^in Seattle. I can't think of his name now. He

speaker: LB
words: wrote a book on libel, which I have someplace. And he said it's lib

speaker: LB
words: elous per se. Checks is different than check. I was sued for $150,000

speaker: LB
words: and the paper was sued for $150,000 in federal court. Well,federal

speaker: LB
words: court means you - it's between states, between one state and another.

speaker: LB
words: So they cannot sue in a state court because one of the people resides

speaker: LB
words: outside the state of Idaho. Well, it appeared this man moved from

speaker: LB
words: Hayden Lake to Spokane. And so our lawyers tried to prepare as much

speaker: LB
words: as they could for the trial in federal court in Moscow and as a last

speaker: LB
words: resort, about two weeks before the trial we sent a local attorney,

speaker: LB
words: Maury 0'Donald- Maurice 0'Donald, down to Boise to check with the Sec

speaker: LB
words: retary of State's office to look over the incorporation papers. Just

speaker: LB
words: thought we might find something. And we already had dug into this

speaker: LB
words: man's history and he'd been a highbinder in Arizona and Utah and Okla

speaker: LB
words: homa, where he had promoted various stock selling schemes. He was not

speaker: LB
words: a very good individual. We knew all that and we had planned to have

speaker: LB
words: witnesses appear to testify to that effect. But 0'Donald when he went

speaker: LB
words: down to the Secretary of State's office found in the file a letter from

speaker: LB
words: this man written to the clerk of the Secretary of State and he said,

speaker: LB
words: "Don't be surprised that the heading of this letter is coming from

speaker: LB
words: Spokane." He said, "This was only done at the request of my lawyer

speaker: LB
words: that it would be better to sue in federal court, so I have temporarily

speaker: LB
words: moved to Spokane rather than Hayden Lake." So, Mr. 0'Donald got a copy

speaker: LB
words: of that letter and when the case came to trial our attorneys made a

speaker: LB
words: motion that jurisdiction was not correct. There was no jurisdiction

speaker: LB
words: in the federal courts and produced the copy of th^letter to show the

speaker: LB
words: reason why. He had said that he was only moving to Spokane temporarily

speaker: LB
words: for the purposes of bringing suit. The judge threw it out immediately.

speaker: LB
words: And the statute of limitations had expired for bringing the suit in

speaker: LB
words: state's courts, so we were freed. (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: You know that sounds—

speaker: LB
words: That's the life of a newspaperman.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds a bit crazy to me, when there was no malicious intent.

speaker: LB
words: No malicious intent.

speaker: SS
words: You made a mistake that the police had made. Now police make mistakes

speaker: SS
words: all the time.

speaker: LB
words: Of course, truth is the biggest defense against libel. We were going

speaker: LB
words: to produce that naturally. We were going to produce the chief of po

speaker: LB
words: lice and we were going to produce the log from the police records that

speaker: LB
words: there was no malice intended and so forth and so on.

speaker: SS
words: But you weren't that confident?

speaker: LB
words: No, we weren't. We weren't. Who knows what a jury's going to do.

speaker: SS
words: When did this happen? I mean, what decade was it?

speaker: LB
words: Well, it was in the early '60's or late '59.

speaker: SS
words: This other case that you spoke of?

speaker: LB
words: That was in the '50's. I would guess about 1956 or along in there.

speaker: LB
words: But you always run that risk.

speaker: LB
words: But you had nothing in the '20's or '30's. Were there any situations

speaker: LB
words: that — ?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, I was threatened with death, one time! Yes. Someone was going to

speaker: LB
words: kill me.

speaker: SS
words: For doing what?

speaker: LB
words: Running a newsstory.

speaker: SS
words: You didn't run the story?

speaker: LB
words: Certainly. It was a lawyer that threatened me. A lawyer from Lewiston

speaker: LB
words: who is now dead. That's common.

speaker: SS
words: Was the story going to be hurting him?

speaker: LB
words: Well, hurting his client. I can't remember the details of it now.

speaker: LB
words: But he called me on the phone and said if I ran that story he was gonna

speaker: LB
words: kill mei And Isaid, "Well, that's fine, I'm going to run story."

speaker: LB
words: Which we did. And that's the end of it. Nothing ever happened. He

speaker: LB
words: may have lost his temper and said something he didn't mean, I don't

speaker: LB
words: know, but it was still a threat.

speaker: SS
words: But in terms of dealing as a watchdog of government- what would that

speaker: SS
words: usually get down to in a specific, concrete way in the '20's and '30's?

speaker: SS
words: For instance, Prohibition came to my mind, because I know that from

speaker: SS
words: what I've heard, not only here, but in a lot of places, there were

speaker: SS
words: varying degrees of enforcement of Prohibition. It was what it would

speaker: SS
words: seem to me to be mostly a selected enforcement. That's because you

speaker: SS
words: only had a limited amount of resources, but what was the attitude that

speaker: SS
words: the paper would take, say, towards that Prohibition question?

speaker: SS
words: I don't think I ever entered into it. It was unpopular with everybody.

speaker: SS
words: But I recall an instance where we were quite critical of a certain

speaker: SS
words: sheriff at that time who used high-handed methods in arresting people.

speaker: SS
words: Instead of being polite about it he'd bang 'em around. And we criti

speaker: SS
words: cized that very severely. That's a case of watchdog. He reformed.

speaker: SS
words: But shortly after that he was not in office. That's the only one that

speaker: SS
words: comes to my mind right now. It's those things. I had an argument withoh,

speaker: SS
words: a long time ago, I had an argument with the clerk of the District

speaker: SS
words: Court. A reporter reported to me that he was sure we were not getting

speaker: SS
words: all the news of minor things. Divorce actions filed; a marriage license issued. So

speaker: SS
words: I went and talked to the clerk and she said, "Well,

speaker: SS
words: they requested not to have this run." So, I said to her, " ,

speaker: SS
words: you're doing my job. You're deciding what's going in the newspaper.

speaker: SS
words: That's my job. Now, if you want to edit the paper, come on down and

speaker: SS
words: apply." And this clerk saw the wisdom of that and from then on out

speaker: SS
words: nothing was ever withheld. And that individual is a very good friend

speaker: SS
words: of mine today. Very good friend. But she was doing my job, you see,

speaker: SS
words: when she decided that if they asked not to have this divorce in the

speaker: SS
words: paper, why, she just stuck the papers in a drawer where the reporter

speaker: SS
words: wouldn't find . Or a marriage license. And then after that,

speaker: SS
words: always came to me. Dozens of couples have come down to me and said,

speaker: SS
words: "We're going to get married but we don't want it in the paper." And

speaker: SS
words: I'd say, "Maybe one of your parents does. I will not keep it out." I

speaker: SS
words: said, "If you want to keep it out go someplace some little distance.

speaker: SS
words: Go over into Washington or go to St. Maries, where they don't have any

speaker: SS
words: daily paper and where it would not be reported and sent on into Spokane.

speaker: SS
words: Go to Orofino or someplace else if you think it's necessary. If you

speaker: SS
words: get married here, it's going to be in the paper.

speaker: SS
words: This relates to what you were saying to me once when we talked about

speaker: SS
words: the newspaper being the paper of record.

speaker: SS
words: That's right. I think it should be. I think every newspaper should be

speaker: SS
words: a paper of record for it's community. I think it should carry every

speaker: SS
words: court action, whether criminal or civil. Because it should be a re

speaker: SS
words: cord, so that you as historian want to look up something, you can find

speaker: SS
words: it in the paper. You can see what it was about. I think every newspaper

speaker: SS
words: should be a newspaper of record for it's community. Of course, the

speaker: SS
words: New York Times is the nation's newspaper of record. That is why every

speaker: SS
words: speech that the president makes, and this has been going on for years

speaker: SS
words: and years and years, it's published in the New York Times in full.

speaker: SS
words: May take two pages, may take three pages; it's published. Every

speaker: SS
words: speech at the Secretary of State gives, no matter where, it's published

speaker: SS
words: in full in the New York Times. It's the recognized the newspaper of

speaker: SS
words: record for the United States. You can always find it there. Every

speaker: SS
words: act of Congress is reported in the New York Times.

speaker: SS
words: How did you regard social activities as whether or not they were news

speaker: SS
words: worthy of record? What I think of-we take these rural papers for ins

speaker: SS
words: tance, the one in Troy, they would typically record what happened on

speaker: SS
words: Little Bear Ridge and it recorded really, the life of those people.

speaker: SS
words: In a place like Moscow, it was a city.

speaker: LB
words: Well, we did that in the early days; the 1920's, we had correspondents

speaker: LB
words: on Big Bear Ridge and Fix Ridge and Troy and Deary and Bovill and all

speaker: LB
words: those places, yes. Because we had readers in those places. We

speaker: LB
words: wanted to give them something. And they don't have big news. They

speaker: LB
words: don't have important town council meetings, but they do have John Jones

speaker: LB
words: going to Portland to visit his sister. So you put it in. Names make

speaker: LB
words: news.

speaker: SS
words: They qualify in your news of record?

speaker: LB
words: Right. That's news. Names make news.

speaker: SS
words: What about Moscow? Was the standard different for what would be included

speaker: SS
words: for Moscow?

speaker: LB
words: You wouldn t include as many trivial facts in Moscow as you would

speaker: LB
words: the outlying communities. We had bigger news to publish-publicize.

speaker: LB
words: More important news. We ran some of those small things. We ran what

speaker: LB
words: we called a personals column every day. Somebody leave and go to New

speaker: LB
words: York, why, it was two or three lines. The bigger you get, the bigger

speaker: LB
words: the town gets the less you can do that. That's correct.

speaker: SS
words: In terms of political conflict in the '20's and '20's in politics;

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever find yourself, the paper, involved in a big political

speaker: SS
words: issue?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, sometimes a big political issue, like maybe the war in Vietnam as an example.

speaker: LB
words: But we didn't have the war in Vietnam in the early days,

speaker: LB
words: but on big issues of that nature we try to take a s tand. Tried to

speaker: LB
words: publicize it. But we did not do the way it's being done now, recom

speaker: LB
words: mending a candidate for election. I didn't think I was smart enough

speaker: LB
words: to do that. So we just kept that out. Let the people decide. We

speaker: LB
words: didn't try to say that the Democratic Party of Idaho was a better par

speaker: LB
words: ty than the Republican. We were smart enough for that.

speaker: SS
words: You didn't consider the paper was affiliated, say, with the Republican

speaker: SS
words: Party?

speaker: LB
words: No. No. We tried to maintain an independent attitude. Individually

speaker: LB
words: I was a Republican, but I tried to be fair on either side. And as

speaker: LB
words: far as chosing between candidates for state office or county office

speaker: LB
words: or city office, no. Tried to publish each side and make no choice of

speaker: LB
words: our own. I think it's wrong in a small town to try and make the choice.

speaker: LB
words: I think it's wrong now for the newspaper here now, and I suppose they'll

speaker: LB
words: learn this to decide that Joe Doakes would make a better mayor than

speaker: LB
words: somebody else. I don't think the paper has a right to that in a small

speaker: LB
words: town. In a big city where the individuals are not known, it's a lit

speaker: LB
words: tle different story.

speaker: SS
words: What about dealing with questions of policy? What's the right stand

speaker: SS
words: to take on the issue? Did your paper feel that an editoral way was

speaker: SS
words: the proper way to take stands on the issues?

speaker: LB
words: No. No, I didn't. It's too small a town. People are too close. I

speaker: LB
words: don't think anyone on the paper is qualified to do it.

speaker: SS
words: Certainly, today now the editorial policy of the paper seems directed

speaker: SS
words: towards those ends.The policy opinion.

speaker: LB
words: Yes, I know. I don't think they should be too local, because, as I

speaker: LB
words: say, I don't think the editor now anymore than I was as editor is

speaker: LB
words: that brilliant that he can distinguish what's right and what's wrong.

speaker: LB
words: Now your big paper,. 's stand is taken by a policy board. The Portland

speaker: LB
words: Oregonian,. the Seattle Times, the editorial opinions are not the ex

speaker: LB
words: pression of one individual person. They have a daily meeting of four

speaker: LB
words: or five. The editor would say, "Okay, you- what'11 we discuss editorially

speaker: LB
words: today? And they decide and hash it back and forth and then

speaker: LB
words: the editor would say, "Okay, we'll take the stand of so-and-so on

speaker: LB
words: this issue. Bill, you write that editorial." And then discuss some

speaker: LB
words: other issues. They've got an editorial board. But here, in this

speaker: LB
words: small town, just one person, and that one person's no smarter than

speaker: LB
words: you or I.

speaker: SS
words: But you would feel a difference between taking a stand on a local is

speaker: SS
words: sue and taking it on a national issue?

speaker: LB
words: Somewhat, yes. Because you're too close to the local issue. And you

speaker: LB
words: can view objectively a national issue. You can't be objective in a

speaker: LB
words: small town when it is a small town issue. I^believe you can. You re

speaker: LB
words: too close to it. They're friends of yours or they.'re not friends of

speaker: LB
words: yours.

speaker: SS
words: And that in itself could influence your position?

speaker: LB
words: That's right. Definitely.

speaker: SS
words: Rather than the merits.

speaker: LB
words: That's right. If you don't like Joe Doaks, why, it's easy to say, "Oh,

speaker: LB
words: his program's haywire." And if you like Joe Doaks, "Why, he's a fine

speaker: LB
words: fellow, I think he's on the right track." Those things are too close

speaker: LB
words: for a small town.

words: I know it.

speaker: LB
words: Oh, they thought it was popular. I don't know. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Sold papers, do you think?

speaker: LB
words: Maybe, I don't know.

words: Yeah, that was awful. He could have been sued for libel every day!

words: He was that kind of a firebrand; I knew him.

speaker: SS
words: What do you remember him as being like, Orcutts?

speaker: LB
words: Firebrand. He had been here, oh, way early, I don't know, in 1910,

speaker: LB
words: 1911, then he went down to Southern Idaho. Firebrand, I tell you, he

speaker: LB

words: think it was^universal concept of that kind of journalism.

speaker: LB
words: Certainly, sure.

speaker: LB
words: I didn't do it.

speaker: SS
words: I didn't see it in your '20's papers.

speaker: LB
words: No. No. It was earlier than that. In the teen years in 1910 and on.

speaker: LB
words: I think it was moFe or less universal. That's my impression.

words: In fact, I've noticed—

speaker: LB
words: Ben Franklin used to have arguments with other editors all the time!

speaker: LB
words: (Laughter)

words: No.

speaker: LB

words: responsibility. The newspaper takes the responsibility as an institu

words: seen one. Now there may be, but I've never seen them. They aren't in

speaker: LB

words: should be, rather than the opinion of J. Shalliday or Bill Hall. Who

words: cares what Bill Hall thinks? You care what the Lewiston Tribune is

words: representative of this this part of the country. Thinks and believes.

words: Not Bill Hall. And I've never seen any newspaper that had signed edi

speaker: LB
words: torials other than Lewiston. Maybe you have, I haven't.

speaker: SS
words: No. No. I was thinking also of who was on the masthead. I was curious

words: to see who was on the staff. I noticed they didn't—

speaker: LB
words: I don't know that any newspaper carries a full staff.

speaker: SS
words: No, I don't think so.

speaker: SS
words: publisher

speaker: LB

words: nated as the publisher, and possibly the name of the editor and that's

words: it. And with us, up until the time I sold out at the Idahonian, I sold

words: a singular unified voice rather than as a group of individuals agreeing

speaker: LB
words: streets wide enough. That's why you have parking on only one side in

words: so many areas of the residential district. a drawback. And

words: you've got to recognize it. You still see it. Salt Lake City's beautiful. Great, big,

words: wide streets. You've been there, haven't you?

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: LB
words: Big, wide streets. I don't know how wide they are, 120 feet or some

words: thing. Brigham Young had the right idea when he laid it out. He was

speaker: LB

words: ago had narrow streets. It had to widen them.

words: could to the city?

words: think was Tom Mc Call, as governor of Oregon. He said, "Come visit

words: us, but don't stay." Which is very unusual. Most states want to at

speaker: LB
words: tract population and attract business and attract money and attract

words: growth. Just natural.

speaker: SS
words: can say you would like a town to grow, but that doesn't bring people.

words: was going to open up a new area or create a paving district, why, there

words: sidewalks and curbing and all that. And they never were unanymous,

words: that I wonder about, because it seems from that point of view that

words: decisions about it and what will happen in Moscow.

speaker: LB

words: munity and the business community. I don't believe there's any great

words: difference, in those days any more than it is now. There's some op

words: posed to it now. Some people living in the residential area are op

words: posed to the business community, thinking they're making too much

speaker: LB
words: money or this and that. But it's not universal. I t'sfjust small. And

speaker: LB
words: I don't think there's any difference in the 1920's than it is now.

speaker: LB
words: I don't believe so.

words: so are you.

words: that Mr. Hartung never goes to a Chamber of Commerce meeting. And I

words: think that there's a very small proportion, smaller proportion of the

speaker: LB
words: faculty who are interested in the community through the Chamber of

speaker: LB
words: Commerce and through the service clubs than there was in those days.

words: not active in the Chamber of Commerce now, but I know that even at

words: the time, ten years ago, when I retired, I think that, oh, the Univer

words: it.

words: ersity at the state level in those days?

speaker: LB

words: the institution; oh, yes. I think they send somebody down now, I don't

speaker: LB
words: know, I^haven't followed it that closely. But in those days, heavens

words: yes,

words: were earlier. How closely knit was the social structure of the leader

words: ship of Moscow? How closely integrated was it in those days?

speaker: SS
words: that didn't belong to the Chamber of Commerce or a service club and

speaker: SS
words: wasn't active in those, why, an effort was made to get him active.

speaker: SS
words: And I think much more so than now. They'll pay their dues now, a

speaker: SS
words: larger number pay dues, but a fewer percentage, a smaller percentage

speaker: SS
words: take an active role in the Chamber of Commerce. They depend too much

speaker: SS
words: upon the secretary; let him do it. And it's like a small family and

speaker: SS
words: a big family, you've got to cooperate and the businessmen and the pro

speaker: SS
words: fessional men were willing to do more of the work. The secretary, in

speaker: SS
words: those days, was a part-time secretary. They got maybe fifty dollars

words: a month to keep the minutes and do the corresponding and so forth.

words: And so the leaders in the community, professional and business, do

speaker: SS

words: an objective and try to perform to reach that objective. Now, it's

words: was there much consultation among the leadership, would you think, be

words: tween these leading citizens that we were talking about?

speaker: LB

speaker: LB
words: bank crisis, as an example, in 1933 and the late 1932 when we had the

speaker: LB
words: banking crisis as the recession approached banks were failing all over

speaker: LB
words: the country, and two failed in Moscow. The First Trust and Savings

speaker: LB
words: Bank was owned by Haaken Melgarde. And the First National Bank clo

speaker: LB
words: sed and it was a branch of the Old National Bank of Spokane. And the

speaker: LB
words: Idaho State Bank had closed previously, and everyone was fearful just

speaker: LB
words: about the beginning of 1933. Roosevelt went into office in March, in

speaker: LB
words: those days, rather than January, and the leaders of the town got to

speaker: LB
words: gether- my partner was one of 'em, George Lamphere- and they seriously

speaker: LB
words: and meant it, pledged their assets to the First Security Bank. And

speaker: LB
words: they had a great big sign out in front signed by George Laatphere and

speaker: LB
words: Roland Hodgins, "We pledge our assets to this bank security." And

speaker: LB
words: they meant it. There was no run on the bank. And the bank stayed open

speaker: LB
words: until Roosevelt closed all the banks of the nation for a period ofwhat?

speaker: LB
words: two or three weeks? But those men did that. They got together

speaker: LB
words: as a group, not through the amber of Commerce, but just individually.

speaker: LB
words: And about a dozen men who were worth a good share of money apiece,

speaker: LB
words: they pledged their assets to the bank. That's community cooperation.

speaker: SS
words: That is. That's an excellent example of what I'm thinking about.

speaker: LB
words: That happened. That happened. Definitely. I didn't sign it; I

speaker: LB
words: didn't have any money. But my partner did! (Chuckles) With the leaders

speaker: LB
words: of the community, I would guess about a dozen, pledged all their as

speaker: LB
words: sets to the security of that bank, and it stayed open. A run never

speaker: LB
words: developed. That's a great asset to the community. Republicans and

speaker: LB
words: Democrats. My partner was a solid Republican, Hodgins was a

speaker: LB
words: leading Democrat; didn't matter, they wanted to see the bank stay open

speaker: LB
words: and they wanted to see the town free of that fear of collapse.

speaker: SS
words: I understand that Haaken Melgarde was very upset about having to shut

speaker: SS
words: down at all.

speaker: LB
words: Definitely. Definitely.

speaker: SS
words: He felt that that was quite wrong.

speaker: LB
words: Right. He it was wrong, and of course, he had to shut down

speaker: LB
words: when Roosevelt closed all the banks. And I believe that they had an

speaker: LB
words: army of auditors check the banks before they would be allowed to re

speaker: LB
words: open. But Haaken Melgarde kept his open, right to the end.

speaker: SS
words: These banks that had failed in Moscow, did they take many people down

speaker: SS
words: with them?

speaker: LB
words: The first- the State Bank- Moscow State Bank, yes, took quite a few

speaker: LB
words: people down with them, yes. The Idaho First National Bank did not, be

speaker: LB
words: cause it closed in fear of a run. The Old National Bank of Spokane,

speaker: LB
words: it closed and all it's branches closed. And I don't think anybody got

speaker: LB
words: hurt. I'm sure everybody got paid off in full. When the bank reopened

speaker: LB
words: it was shortly after that sold to the Idaho First National Bank and

speaker: LB
words: instead of being a branch of the Old National Bank of Spokane it be

speaker: LB
words: came a branch of the Idaho First National/ But I don't think

speaker: LB
words: anybody ever lost a dime by that bank's closing. They closed to pre

speaker: LB
words: vent a run on the bank and prevent anyone being damaged.

speaker: SS
words: The Moscow State Bank, it hurt?

speaker: LB
words: It hurt, yes, it hurt. I was banking there and all I ever got back

words: was about fifty cents on the dollar. I didn't have much to lose in

speaker: LB
words: those days, so it didn't matter much.

speaker: SS
words: Still—

speaker: LB
words: Still, you lose half of what you've got in there. They paid out rough

speaker: LB
words: ly fifty cents on the dollar, over a period of a couple of years, un

speaker: LB
words: til they could get their money bank in on loans and so forth.

words: collar people, people definitely not well-off before the Depression

words: hit? Were they in real bad shape?

words: of the country. I think they were pretty good shape. Wages were quite

words: stoker of the old Star Mirror as an advertising stunt, just to publi

words: cize it. Didn't burn very well, but we took some eighteen-cent wheat

words: was the price on the local market, and fed it into the stoker.

speaker: LB
words: Mixed a little coal with it and it burned] (Laughter) Just as a pro

speaker: LB
words: motion stunt, to show that we were trying to help the farmers to get

speaker: LB
words: rid of the surplus- burdensome - surplus of wheat. The farmers were

speaker: LB
words: the ones that were hurt. A lot of institutions foreclosed on the farms,

speaker: LB
words: they couldn't pay off their mortgages and a lot of farms were foreclosed,

words: I've heard so much about the city- people in big cities- really having

words: a rough time. I had figured- well, the farmers who told me are still

speaker: SS
words: here, those that held out were in fairly good shape because of what

words: they could grow on their farms.

speaker: LB
words: Right, uh-huh.

words: But I was wondering- to me, Moscow, seemed like a kind of inbetween

speaker: SS
words: place and not a big city.

words: We didn't have any industry. People who worked were people who worked

words: in the stores the university. There was no industry any more

speaker: LB
words: than there is now. We have no industry.

words: Those people were not laid off.

speaker: LB
words: No, they were not laid off. No, not to any extent. The only industry

words: we have now is the industry of building,^plumbing trade, the electri

speaker: LB
words: cian trade, the carpentwl trade. That's it. You've no industry. Don't

speaker: LB
words: have any blue collars, except service industries, more or less.

speaker: SS
words: Like the clerks in the stores.

speaker: LB
words: Clerks in the stores, they were not laid off. No, the town was in

speaker: LB
words: good shape. In better shape than other towns in Idaho because more

speaker: LB
words: Twin Falls, so might as well go to the University. The enrollment in

speaker: LB
words: creased. Definitely. Definitely, enrollment increased because of the

speaker: LB
words: Depression. Student couldn't get a job, why, he'd come to the University. He had a

speaker: LB

speaker: LB
words: Well, Mr. Kelly, one of his ideas was that athletics should be amateur

speaker: LB
words: completely. No athlete was entitled to any more than any other in

speaker: LB
words: dividual. IN those days the athletes would work for say, fifty dollars

words: a month. They would work around the gymnasium. They would set the

speaker: LB

speaker: LB
words: other chores. Or they would work for a dormitory as a hasher and get

speaker: LB
words: paid that way. But they worked. There was nothing given to them free.

speaker: LB
words: They had to work for it. Kelly's idea was that if you were an athlete

speaker: LB
words: he should not even get paid for working, and they should get no help

speaker: LB
words: at all. The coaches were quite disturbed. So, informally, a little

speaker: LB
words: group formed downtown to raise some money for athletes. And I was

speaker: LB
words: treasurer of what money we raised. Went up and down Main Street and

speaker: LB
words: we raised probably all told, maybe, $2,500 or $3,000. And I was treasurer

speaker: LB
words: at the Star Mirror, and we kept our money in the Moscow State

speaker: LB
words: Bank, which was right down the street on the corner. And the coach

speaker: LB
words: would give a football player or a basketball player a requisition to

speaker: LB
words: me to pay him thirty dollars for work performed in moving chairs or

speaker: LB
words: sweeping out the gym or washing towels or what have you, or raking the

speaker: LB
words: lawn. And they did the work and I would keep that requisition and

speaker: LB
words: give the boy a check and he would cash it. Well, Earl David was one

speaker: LB
words: of the leaders of the movement, he's been dead now about ten, twelve

speaker: LB
words: years, Abe Goff was another, and I think it was Earl David, I'm pretty

speaker: LB
words: sure, that said, — of course, we did have at that time, as we still

speaker: LB
words: have now, we were in the Pacific Coast Conference, and they had a com

speaker: LB
words: missioner. And all aid given to athletes, whether they worked for it

speaker: LB
words: or it was strictly aid, had to go through the University Bursar's of

speaker: LB
words: fice, to make it legal. Any under -the-counter payments " an ath

speaker: LB
words: lete was wrong. So the commissioner would come once a year and go

speaker: LB
words: through the books and ask questions around town and see if he could

speaker: LB
words: find out whether we were abiding by the rules. So we thought what

speaker: LB
words: we were doing might be- come under suspicion. So we decided we'd

speaker: LB
words: hide the money. And because Mr. Kelly was so adamant no athlete would

speaker: LB
words: ever get any pay of any kind, he said, "Well, let's put this in the

speaker: LB
words: name of the Kelly Estate!!" It was Earl David's idea. So we opened

speaker: LB
words: an account at the Moscow State Bank under the title the Kelly Estate!

speaker: LB
words: And I was treasurer and I wrote the check on the Kelly Estate as a

speaker: LB
words: joke by President Fred Kelly. And then, of course, then the Moscow

speaker: LB
words: State Bank closed, oh, in January, I believe of '33. And our money

speaker: LB
words: was tied up and we had about $1,000 left in that. We spent most of

speaker: LB
words: it for football, you see, in the fall. We had about $1,000 in the

speaker: LB
words: bank. So everybody who had money in the bank, I had my money there,

speaker: LB
words: filed a form to get your money back when the bank was liquidated. So

speaker: LB
words: I filed a form for my own personal account and I filed a form for the

speaker: LB
words: Kelly Estate. And the bank examiner who came here from Boise to li

speaker: LB
words: quidate the bank said, "Well, that's fine. I've got to have your autho

speaker: LB
words: rity to act for the Kelly Estate. Get me your certified copy of your

speaker: LB
words: appointment as administrator or executor of the estate and I want a

speaker: LB
words: copy of any wills that were involved and so forth." And I tried to

speaker: LB
words: explain that it was not an estate, and the examiner couldn't under

speaker: LB
words: stand why it was not an estate. It was the Kelly Estate. I argued

speaker: LB

speaker: LB

speaker: LB

speaker: LB

speaker: LB

speaker: LB

speaker: LB

speaker: LB

words: Can you speculate for me, show a little light on why that is so, that

speaker: SS
words: Moscow seems to be such a much more dynamic as a business community

speaker: SS
words: than Pullman?

speaker: LB
words: Well, it's the fact that we had a more vigorous bunch of merchants.

speaker: LB
words: Merchants who were more aggressive and built up their own businesses

speaker: LB
words: a big department store, which is now the"fjatuna J^artments. And he was

speaker: LB
words: a vigorous merchandiser. We had David's store, which was a vigorour

speaker: LB
words: merchandizer. And Franklin David and later his sons, who were all in

speaker: LB
words: the business, and they were competitors; promoters. And Pullman just

speaker: LB
words: never had any good, solid merchants. They were lackadaisical. They

speaker: LB
words: go out and push and compete, so^they didn t grow and we did.

speaker: LB
words: That's why we got the shopping center west of town and Pullman didn't.

speaker: LB
words: It was a cinch two years ago that the first one to start it was going

speaker: LB
words: to get it. So, this one started first. The merchants here don't like

speaker: LB
words: it, but I think it's going to help the town. My viewpoint is that it'll

speaker: LB
words: help the town. My viewpoint is that if I was going to have a restau

speaker: LB
words: rant I'd want to be right next door to the other restaurant. Attract

speaker: LB
words: the trade. So I think the stores out there will attract shoppers from

speaker: LB
words: to attract trade. We had, when I first came here, Mr. Williamsonjhad

speaker: LB
words: a wider area and the merchants on downtown Main Street are going to

speaker: LB
words: get the fringe benefits. If they can't find what they want out there

speaker: LB
words: they're going to come downtown here and find it. The more traffic

speaker: LB
words: you've got the more merchandise you're going to sell. That's going

speaker: LB
words: to be an asset to the community. And I think it'll be an asset to

speaker: LB
words: every retail merchant in town.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that they initially have a depressing effect on some of

speaker: SS
words: the townspeople?

speaker: LB
words: I don't think so, I don't think so. I could be wrong, but I don't

speaker: LB
words: think so because, as I say, - any businessman will say he wants to be

speaker: LB
words: where his competitor is. As close to him as he can.

speaker: SS
words: The only question I have about it- the thing I wonder is the size of

speaker: SS
words: the potential market. If there aren't going to be enough people coming

speaker: SS
words: in because of what they are offered to come into Moscow, it will affect

speaker: SS
words: the competitive effect of those stores.

speaker: LB
words: I think it will, because people from Moscow now go down to Lewiston

speaker: LB
words: to shop at K-Mart. They have a K Mart at Lewiston and they g o down

speaker: LB
words: there to shop down there because they have bigger stores and more stores

speaker: LB
words: And they will drive that thirty-nine miles. Well, now with this thing

speaker: LB
words: out here ti's going to bring people from a wider area. More are going

speaker: LB
words: to come to Moscow to shop from Palouse than came before because they'll

speaker: LB
words: have K Mart out there, they'll have Payless Drugstore and we've got

speaker: LB
words: the Drug Fair here. It's greater selection, more choice, so they'll

speaker: LB
words: come from farther away. I think more people will come from Colfax to

speaker: LB
words: Moscow and Palouse and Genesee and around. It'll take certain trade

speaker: LB
words: from south of here that now goes to Lewiston will come to Moscow; it's

speaker: LB
words: shorter.

speaker: SS
words: It strikes me that Moscow has much more stability as a city than Pullman does.

speaker: SS
words: Between the two towns there is much more continuity here.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I think part of that is the fact that Washington State University

speaker: SS
words: has a greater impact upon Pullman than the University of Idaho had upon

speaker: SS
words: Moscow. Because Washington State College is just double the size of

speaker: SS
words: the University of Idaho and I think it overshadows the town to a grea

speaker: SS
words: ter extent, than it does the University. Overshadowing it they've been

speaker: SS
words: more active- the faculty of WSU has been more active in politics; in

speaker: SS
words: running the City Council and overshadowing the town in the service clubs

speaker: SS
words: The Kiwanis and Rotary and so forth. They dominate to a greater extent,

speaker: SS
words: and that hurts the town.

speaker: SS
words: Then Moscow has a much better balance as a service community; in Moscow

speaker: SS
words: it's very strong.

speaker: LB
words: Yes, I think that's correct.

speaker: LB
words: Have I helped you?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, a great deal. I think of one more thing that I'd like to ask you

speaker: SS
words: about. As you went onWith your newspaper work; did you find that your

speaker: SS
words: education on the job to learn journalism?

speaker: LB
words: No. Oh, as a senior in high school I was a parttime reporter for a

speaker: LB
words: Boise newspaper. A senior in high school, and I worked for that news

speaker: LB
words: paper, the old Capitol News during the summer before I came to the uni

speaker: LB
words: versity. And I liked it and as soon as I got up here I started working

speaker: LB
words: on the Argonaut. And after about a year, we were getting into the re

speaker: LB
words: cession of the '20's, I realized that I was a burden upon my dad and

speaker: LB
words: so I free-lanced. And I got to be a correspondent for half a dozen

speaker: LB
words: newspapers.

speaker: SS
words: Is that the way you put yourself through school?

speaker: LB
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Free-lance newspaper work?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, that is correct.

speaker: SS
words: I didn't realize that.

speaker: LB
words: I wrote for the Salt Lake Tribune, the Portland Oregonian, the Seattle

speaker: LB
words: Times, Spokane Chronicle, the Oregon Journal, the Seattle PI. Had

speaker: LB
words: both newspapers in Seattle so I'd write two stories. And I wrote for

speaker: LB
words: the Christian Science Monitor. As a senior, in my last year at the

speaker: LB
words: university I was averaging three hundred dollars a month income. And

speaker: LB
words: wages were thirty-five cents an hour. I made more money then than I

speaker: LB
words: made after I left here, by far. I took a job for $100 a month. But

speaker: LB
words: I made $300 a month last year in college.^ So I had training.

speaker: SS
words: And a long-standing interest.

speaker: LB
words: Long-standing interest. I think I told you before I went.to the Westinghouse

speaker: LB
words: Company in the publicity department as a ghostwriter and then

speaker: LB
words: got disgusted with Pittsburgh and came out to Boise and got a job on

speaker: LB
words: the Idaho Statesman and then the chance came to come up here.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know what attracted you to journalism?

speaker: LB
words: No. Have no idea. Just liked it. Better than I liked engineering.

speaker: SS
words: That's what you said before.
